text.title: roadmap-design Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for roadmap-design. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Choose the format by uncertainty level first, then external-sharing requirement. High uncertainty + external sharing = outcome-based or Now-Next-Later (themes only). Low uncertainty + external sharing = timeline.
rule.rationale: Format mismatch is the root cause of roadmap trust failures. A timeline roadmap in a high-uncertainty context creates commitments that cannot be kept, destroying stakeholder trust on every slip.

rule.statement: For a solo founder, the roadmap must fit on one screen. If it does not, it is a backlog with a roadmap header, not a roadmap.
rule.rationale: A roadmap communicates strategic direction. Anything requiring scrolling has too much detail for strategic communication and too little for task execution.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Gather four inputs before touching the roadmap format: company/product vision, business goals for the period, user research insights, and technical constraints. Output: clear objectives for the roadmap period.
rule.rationale: A roadmap without strategy inputs is a backlog sorted by recency. The objectives from this step are the connective tissue between every initiative and the business direction.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Use the selection matrix as a deterministic first step. Do not let the audience or personal preference drive the format choice — let uncertainty level and external-sharing requirement drive it.
rule.rationale: Format chosen by audience preference produces a timeline roadmap for high-uncertainty work because stakeholders always want dates. The matrix overrides this bias.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Group all planned work into 3–5 themes. For each theme write: name, which objective it drives, why it is prioritised now, and 2–3 example initiatives. No theme should be orphaned from an objective.
rule.rationale: Themes without objectives are backlog categories, not strategic groupings. The objective link is what prevents "Later" from becoming a permanent graveyard of low-priority features.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Assign each initiative a confidence level: High (committed and scoped), Medium (planned, needs scoping), Low (exploring, tentative). At least 30% of items must be Medium or Low — if everything is High, the labelling is decoration.
rule.rationale: Uniform High confidence strips the signal from Now-Next-Later. Stakeholders learn to ignore confidence levels that are never Low, and the roadmap loses its communication function.

rule.statement: Limit Now to 5 initiatives maximum. Past 5, the team is effectively planning a backlog sprint, not a strategic now-horizon.
rule.rationale: Teams can execute 3–5 significant initiatives in parallel before coordination costs outweigh benefits. More than 5 in Now signals that priorities have not been made.

rule.statement: Step 5 — For each horizon, document: why you chose these initiatives, what you are NOT doing and why, dependencies and risks, and how success will be measured. The "not doing" section is mandatory — it stops scope creep better than any priority list.
rule.rationale: Without explicit exclusions, every reasonable idea becomes a candidate for this quarter. The not-doing list also creates a retrospective record of scope decisions, which is invaluable for explaining past trade-offs.

rule.statement: Every initiative must link to one objective ID. Initiatives without an objective link are strategy leakage — remove them or create the missing objective.
rule.rationale: Orphaned initiatives represent work that is in the plan without strategic justification. They accumulate over time and eventually dominate the roadmap without anyone being able to explain why.

rule.statement: Update cadence: monthly review of the initiative list, quarterly rewrite of the full document. Never rewrite the whole doc each sprint — that destroys the historical diff signal.
rule.rationale: Quarter-to-quarter diffs are the primary retrospective artefact. Frequent complete rewrites make it impossible to see how the plan evolved or why certain items moved.

rule.statement: Always include an "Explicitly Not Doing" section with reasons. It doubles as a future review log for stakeholder questions like "why didn't we build X?"
rule.rationale: Stakeholders re-request excluded items constantly. A documented reason closes each request with a reference instead of reopening the debate.

rule.statement: Maintain two artefacts: internal roadmap (full detail: outcomes, owners, confidence, metrics) and external roadmap (themes and benefits only, no confidence levels, no owners, no metric targets). Sync via script, not copy-paste.
rule.rationale: Sharing confidence levels externally signals uncertainty in a way that customers interpret as incompetence. Sharing metric targets creates contractual expectations. Sharing owners exposes internal structure.
